intro: Food is where the enhancement-versus-generation line is sharpest: improving a real photo is broadly accepted, generating a dish from nothing gets called out as false advertising in public, fast. The Forkable case below is the anchor story for this category.

trends.title: Delivery platforms deploy AI photo enhancement at scale
trends.body: Uber Eats announced AI tools that enhance low-quality restaurant photos, lighting, resolution, framing, even re-plating a takeout-box shot onto a proper dish, plus AI-written menu descriptions. DoorDash launched comparable AI photo tools in April 2025.

trends.title: Enhancement is tolerated, generation is not
trends.body: Reporting on the delivery-platform rollout draws a consistent line: improving a photo of a real dish is broadly accepted by diners, but generating the dish from scratch triggers false-advertising accusations on social media almost immediately.

trends.title: A niche SaaS layer sells generated menu photography directly to restaurants
trends.body: Tools like FoodShot AI, FoodPhoto.ai, and MenuPhotoAI sell generated or enhanced menu imagery to restaurants that can't afford a proper food photography shoot.

trends.title: Fully AI-generated restaurant advertising has arrived
trends.body: Restaurant Business reported that Teriyaki Madness ran an ad campaign built almost entirely with AI, generated actors, backdrops, and voiceover, with the food itself shot for real, described as a fast-casual first.

trends.title: A claimed collapse in stock food photography sales
trends.body: A single non-mainstream analysis claims zero traditional food photographs made Adobe Stock's top-seller lists between April and August 2025, with roughly 91 percent of top-selling food imagery now AI-made. This is a single, non-mainstream source and should be treated as indicative at most.

tools.name: Uber Eats AI photo tools
tools.note: Built-in enhancement + re-plating for merchant photos

tools.name: Typeface
tools.note: AI imagery for food and beverage brands

campaigns.title: Quietly replaced real restaurant photos sitewide, got caught, reverted
campaigns.body: The San Francisco catering platform Forkable swapped real restaurant photos for AI-generated ones across its site without telling the restaurants involved. Owners found out after launch; a co-founder later admitted the company moved too quickly and reverted to real photography. The clearest cautionary case in this category.

campaigns.title: Brand-side AI food imagery in marketing
campaigns.body: Burger King's Million Dollar Whopper campaign and Heinz's earlier AI Ketchup campaign both used AI-generated imagery in social and poster creative as a deliberate marketing device rather than a cost-saving substitution.

controversies.title: False advertising accusations
controversies.body: Diners calling out generated menu photos as misrepresentation is now a recurring social-media story, not an edge case.

controversies.title: Platforms police AI in both directions
controversies.body: Uber Eats asks customers not to submit AI-generated photos to its own community-photo program, while DoorDash permanently banned a driver in December 2025 for submitting an AI-faked proof-of-delivery photo.

controversies.title: The wider ecosystem loses work too
controversies.body: Food stylists, prop stylists, and suppliers are affected alongside photographers when a shoot is skipped entirely, though high-end editorial and cookbook work remains human-led for now. Source domain has since gone offline, treat as general industry commentary rather than a live citation.
